primary_dx: Foregut Duplication Cyst, Bronchogenic-Type, With Focal Gastric-Type Mucosa And Prominent Smooth Muscle Wall
differential_dx: Cystic Degeneration In Smooth Muscle Tumor Or Less Likely Cystic Spindle-Cell GIST

microscopic_findings.text: Cyst lining shows **pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium** with goblet cells, supporting **respiratory-type foregut lining**.
Separate foci contain **gastric-type mucinous glands** embedded within or adjacent to the **smooth muscle wall**.
The cyst wall is composed of intersecting fascicles of **bland smooth muscle** with **blunt-ended nuclei** and eosinophilic cytoplasm.
There is prominent **hemorrhage**, edema, and mixed inflammation with luminal debris, but **no tumor necrosis or mitotic atypia**.

interpretation: The combination of **respiratory-type ciliated epithelium**, focal **gastric-type mucosa**, and a thick **smooth muscle wall** supports **foregut duplication cyst / foregut cyst, bronchogenic-type**. The spindle-cell component is cytologically bland and is best interpreted as smooth muscle of the cyst wall with reactive leiomyomatous proliferation rather than a primary spindle-cell neoplasm. **Cystic mesothelioma** is disfavored because the lining is not mesothelial, and **GIST/leiomyoma with cystic change** is less favored because a true heterogeneous foregut epithelial lining is present.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells with a wall composed of dense fibrous tissue and smooth muscle. There is extensive hemorrhage and inflammation within the wall and surrounding tissue.

The most likely diagnosis is a cystic lesion of the gastrointestinal tract, such as a cystic leiomyoma or a cystic gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST).

Differential diagnoses include a cystic leiomyoma, which would show smooth muscle bundles in the wall, and a cystic GIST, which would typically show spindle cells or epithelioid cells in the wall.
tiles.rationale: Mid portion of main tissue to identify hypercellular areas at low power

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by columnar epithelium with papillary infoldings, surrounded by a thick wall composed of smooth muscle bundles and fibrous tissue. The lumen contains amorphous material and hemorrhage.

The most likely diagnosis is a cystic lesion of the gastrointestinal tract, such as a cystic gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) or a cystic leiomyoma.

Differential diagnoses include a cystic GIST, which would typically show spindle cells or epithelioid cells in the wall, and a cystic leiomyoma, which would show smooth muscle bundles in the wall.
tiles.rationale: Global low-power overview of dominant mass architecture.

tiles.description:
The image shows a proliferation of spindle cells arranged in intersecting fascicles with abundant extravasated red blood cells. The spindle cells have elongated, wavy nuclei and eosinophilic cytoplasm.

The most likely diagnosis is a leiomyoma with hemorrhage. Differential diagnoses include gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) and schwannoma, which can be distinguished by immunohistochemistry (e.g., CD117, DOG1 for GIST; S100 for schwannoma).
tiles.rationale: Left-central area to look for cellular nodules vs collagenous zones

tiles.description:
The image shows a mass composed of dense fibrous tissue with prominent, thick-walled blood vessels and areas of hemorrhage. The mass is attached to surrounding adipose tissue and skeletal muscle.

The most likely diagnosis is a fibrovascular polyp. Differential diagnoses include a leiomyoma, which would show more prominent smooth muscle bundles, and a gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST), which would typically have a more cellular appearance and potentially show spindle or epithelioid cells.
tiles.rationale: Assess left side for GI wall layers/serosa and interface.

tiles.description:
The image shows a proliferation of spindle cells arranged in intersecting fascicles with elongated, wavy nuclei and eosinophilic cytoplasm. There is also prominent hemorrhage and areas of necrosis.

The most likely diagnosis is a gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST). The spindle cell morphology and fascicular arrangement are characteristic features. Differential diagnoses include leiomyoma/leiomyosarcoma, schwannoma, and solitary fibrous tumor, which can be distinguished by their specific immunohistochemical profiles.
tiles.rationale: Low-power look at left portion for distinct nodules, lymphoid cuff, or glandular elements.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells, with a wall composed of dense fibrous tissue and smooth muscle. The lumen contains amorphous material and red blood cells.

The most likely diagnosis is a cystic lesion of the gastrointestinal tract, such as a cystic lymphangioma or a mesothelial cyst.

Differential diagnoses include a cystic lymphangioma, which would typically show lymphatic channels within the wall, and a mesothelial cyst, which would show a lining of mesothelial cells.
tiles.rationale: Right-upper area where tissue seems denser on thumbnail

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by columnar epithelium with papillary projections and a wall composed of fibrous tissue and smooth muscle. The surrounding tissue includes adipose tissue and dense fibrous tissue.

The most likely diagnosis is a cystic lesion of the gastrointestinal tract, such as a cystic adenoma or cystic neoplasm. The presence of columnar epithelium with papillary projections suggests a glandular origin.

Differential diagnoses include a cystic adenoma of the stomach or duodenum, or a cystic neoplasm of the pancreas or biliary tract. The location and attachment to the stomach and adrenal gland would be important factors in distinguishing between these possibilities.
tiles.rationale: Assess right side for capsule, nodularity, hemorrhage/necrosis.

tiles.description:
The image shows mature adipose tissue with scattered small blood vessels and areas of hemorrhage. The adipocytes are uniform with clear cytoplasm and peripherally located nuclei.

The most likely diagnosis is a lipoma. Differential diagnoses include a liposarcoma, which would show atypical adipocytes, lipoblasts, or increased cellularity, and a myelolipoma, which would contain hematopoietic elements.
tiles.rationale: Separate small region 1 tissue fragment to assess if more cellular

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by pseudostratified columnar epithelium with cilia and goblet cells, surrounded by fibrous tissue and smooth muscle. The lumen contains amorphous material and debris.

The most likely diagnosis is a foregut duplication cyst. This diagnosis is supported by the presence of a cystic structure lined by respiratory-type epithelium (pseudostratified columnar with cilia and goblet cells) and the surrounding fibrous and smooth muscle wall, which are characteristic features of foregut duplication cysts. The location in the left upper quadrant, attached to the adrenal gland and stomach, is also consistent with this diagnosis.

Differential diagnoses include a bronchogenic cyst, which is a type of foregut duplication cyst, and a cystic teratoma. A bronchogenic cyst would also be lined by respiratory-type epithelium, but teratomas typically contain elements from all three germ layers, such as skin, cartilage, or neural tissue, which are not seen in this image.
tiles.rationale: Low-power look at right portion for vascular/patternless areas or hemorrhage/necrosis.

tiles.description:
The image shows mature adipose tissue with areas of hemorrhage and inflammation. The adipocytes are uniform with no atypia.

The most likely diagnosis is a benign lipoma. Differential diagnoses include a liposarcoma, which would show atypical adipocytes and lipoblasts, and an adrenal cortical adenoma, which would have cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei.
tiles.rationale: Target Region 2 small focus—rule out accessory spleen/pancreatic rest or diagnostic glandular elements.

tiles.description:
The image shows a mass composed of mature adipose tissue, dense fibrous tissue, and smooth muscle bundles, with areas of hemorrhage and inflammation. The presence of mature adipose tissue, smooth muscle, and fibrous tissue in a mass from the left upper quadrant is characteristic of a benign mesenchymal neoplasm.

The most likely diagnosis is a benign mesenchymal neoplasm, such as a lipoma or fibrolipoma, given the presence of mature adipose tissue and fibrous tissue. A leiomyoma could also be considered if the smooth muscle component is predominant.

Differential diagnoses include a lipoma, fibrolipoma, or leiomyoma. A lipoma would be composed predominantly of mature adipose tissue. A fibrolipoma would have a significant component of fibrous tissue in addition to adipose tissue. A leiomyoma would be composed predominantly of smooth muscle bundles.
tiles.rationale: Inferior aspect for attached fat/serosa and any mucosa or submucosa.

tiles.description:
The image shows a proliferation of spindle cells with elongated, blunt-ended nuclei arranged in fascicles, along with prominent interstitial hemorrhage. The spindle cells are consistent with smooth muscle.

The most likely diagnosis is a leiomyoma.

Differential diagnoses include gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) and schwannoma. GISTs typically show more cellular pleomorphism and may have a different growth pattern. Schwannomas often have Verocay bodies and a different immunohistochemical profile.
tiles.rationale: Hypercellular spindle cell fascicular area for cytology/mitoses

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells with a wall composed of dense fibrous tissue and smooth muscle. The lumen contains amorphous material, red blood cells, and inflammatory cells.

The most likely diagnosis is a cystic lesion of the gastrointestinal tract, such as a cystic lymphangioma or a cystic mesothelial cyst.

Differential diagnoses include a cystic lymphangioma, which would typically show lymphatic channels lined by endothelial cells, and a cystic mesothelial cyst, which would show mesothelial lining cells.
tiles.rationale: Cyst wall area with smooth muscle/fibrous tissue to assess lining and cellular component

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by columnar epithelium with underlying stroma containing a mixed inflammatory infiltrate and numerous foamy macrophages. The lumen contains amorphous material and cellular debris.

The most likely diagnosis is a cystic lesion of the gastrointestinal tract, such as a gastric duplication cyst or a cystic teratoma.

Differential diagnoses include a cystic teratoma, which would typically show additional tissue types (e.g., squamous epithelium, cartilage, neural tissue) not seen in this image, and a gastric duplication cyst, which is a congenital lesion lined by gastric-type epithelium.
tiles.rationale: Evaluate cyst wall at higher power for smooth muscle, glands, cartilage, seromucinous glands; exclude endometriosis.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened to cuboidal epithelium, surrounded by dense fibrous tissue with chronic inflammation and hemorrhage. The wall contains smooth muscle bundles and adipose tissue.

The most likely diagnosis is a cystic lesion of the gastrointestinal tract, such as a duplication cyst or a mesenteric cyst. The presence of smooth muscle and adipose tissue in the wall supports a mesenteric origin. Differential diagnoses include a duplication cyst, which typically has a muscular wall, and a mesenteric cyst, which is often lined by flattened epithelium and contains adipose tissue.
tiles.rationale: Better define central cystic/duct-like spaces and surrounding wall composition.

tiles.description:
The image shows a hypocellular lesion with scattered spindle cells embedded in a prominent myxoid matrix. Numerous small, round, eosinophilic structures are also present.

The most likely diagnosis is a myxoma. Differential diagnoses include myxoid neurofibroma and myxoid liposarcoma, which can be distinguished by the presence of S100 positivity and lipoblasts, respectively.
tiles.rationale: Right-upper cystic structure: evaluate lining, any atypia or vascular/lymphatic nature

tiles.description: The image shows a disorganized proliferation of mature adipose tissue, smooth muscle, and fibrous tissue with prominent hemorrhage and scattered dark granular material. The presence of mature adipose tissue and smooth muscle in a disorganized pattern is characteristic of a mesenchymal hamartoma.
tiles.rationale: Assess fibrovascular area with thick vessels; look for organoid/nested vs spindle.

tiles.description:
The image shows a proliferation of spindle cells arranged in intersecting fascicles with elongated, blunt-ended nuclei and abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm. There is also prominent interstitial hemorrhage.

The most likely diagnosis is a leiomyoma. Differential diagnoses include gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) and schwannoma, which can be distinguished by immunohistochemistry (e.g., CD117, DOG1 for GIST; S100 for schwannoma).
tiles.rationale: Assess spindle cell areas for schwannoma vs SFT vs GIST patterns.

tiles.description:
The image shows bundles of smooth muscle cells with elongated, blunt-ended nuclei and abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm, arranged in fascicles. There is also prominent interstitial hemorrhage.

The most likely diagnosis is a leiomyoma. Differential diagnoses include gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) and schwannoma, which can be distinguished by immunohistochemistry (e.g., CD117, DOG1 for GIST; S100 for schwannoma).
tiles.rationale: Adjacent area near spindle focus to look for different architecture (palisading, vessels)

tiles.description:
The image shows a mass composed of dense fibrous tissue with scattered chronic inflammation and dark granular material. The mass is attached to the stomach wall by filamentous adhesions.

The most likely diagnosis is a fibrous pseudotumor. Differential diagnoses include a fibrous scar or a desmoid-type fibromatosis, which would typically show more cellular spindle cells and infiltrative growth.
tiles.rationale: Assess right-sided cyst lining/papillae; look for mucin, glands, atypia.

tiles.description:
The image shows a spindle cell proliferation with elongated nuclei and eosinophilic cytoplasm, arranged in fascicles. There is also a prominent inflammatory infiltrate and areas of hemorrhage.

The most likely diagnosis is a gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST).

Differential diagnoses include leiomyoma, schwannoma, and inflammatory myofibroblastic tumor. Leiomyoma would typically show more uniform spindle cells with blunt-ended nuclei and less inflammation. Schwannoma would have Antoni A and B areas and Verocay bodies. Inflammatory myofibroblastic tumor would have a more prominent inflammatory component and myofibroblastic spindle cells.
tiles.rationale: High power of lining epithelium type (ciliated? gastric? intestinal?) and underlying stroma.

tiles.description:
The image shows a mixture of mature adipose tissue and bundles of smooth muscle. The smooth muscle bundles are arranged in fascicles and are intermixed with the fat.

The most likely diagnosis is a leiomyolipoma, a benign mesenchymal tumor composed of mature adipose tissue and smooth muscle.

Differential diagnoses include lipoma (composed only of mature adipose tissue) and leiomyoma (composed only of smooth muscle). The presence of both components in this lesion is characteristic of leiomyolipoma.
tiles.rationale: Inferior fat/fibrous/smooth muscle region—evaluate for GI wall layers or desmoid-like pattern.

tiles.description:
The image shows a proliferation of spindle cells with elongated, blunt-ended nuclei arranged in intersecting fascicles. There is also prominent interstitial hemorrhage and scattered inflammatory cells.

The most likely diagnosis is a benign smooth muscle tumor, such as a leiomyoma.

Differential diagnoses include gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) and schwannoma. GISTs typically show more cellular pleomorphism and may have a different growth pattern. Schwannomas often have Verocay bodies and a more prominent lymphocytic infiltrate.
tiles.rationale: High power of spindle cells: mitoses, palisading, perinuclear vacuoles, skeinoid fibers.

tiles.description:
The image shows a proliferation of spindle cells with elongated, blunt-ended nuclei arranged in intersecting fascicles. There is also prominent interstitial hemorrhage.

The most likely diagnosis is a leiomyoma. Differential diagnoses include gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) and schwannoma, which can be distinguished by immunohistochemistry (e.g., CD117, DOG1 for GIST; S100 for schwannoma).
tiles.rationale: Sample another cellular fascicular area for mitotic count/atypia variability

tiles.description:
The image shows mature adipose tissue with areas of hemorrhage and scattered inflammatory cells. The adipocytes are uniform with no atypia.

The most likely diagnosis is a benign lipoma. The differential diagnosis includes a well-differentiated liposarcoma, which would show atypical adipocytes and lipoblasts, and a lipoma-like angiomyolipoma, which would contain thick-walled blood vessels and smooth muscle.
tiles.rationale: Confirm region 2 is fat/hemorrhage vs accessory spleen/pancreatic acini/ducts.

tiles.description:
The image shows a mass composed of dense fibrous tissue with prominent smooth muscle bundles, interspersed adipose tissue, and areas of hemorrhage. Scattered dark granular material is also present.

The most likely diagnosis is a fibrovascular polyp of the stomach.

Differential diagnoses include a leiomyoma, which would show more uniform smooth muscle bundles, and a gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST), which would typically have a more cellular appearance and may show spindle or epithelioid morphology.
tiles.rationale: Characterize cyst lining epithelium and wall (smooth muscle vs fibrous), look for GI mucosa/duplication cyst features.

tiles.description:
The image shows bundles of smooth muscle fibers with elongated, blunt-ended nuclei, interspersed with mature adipose tissue and areas of hemorrhage. The smooth muscle bundles are arranged in fascicles.

The most likely diagnosis is a leiomyolipoma, a benign mesenchymal tumor composed of smooth muscle and adipose tissue.

Differential diagnoses include a lipoma with entrapped smooth muscle and a gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) with fatty change. A lipoma would lack the prominent smooth muscle component, while a GIST would typically show spindle cells with different nuclear features and often express CD117 and DOG1.
tiles.rationale: Look for epithelioid component or perivascular growth pattern near hemorrhagic zones

tiles.description:
The image shows a mixture of mature adipose tissue, smooth muscle bundles, and fibrous tissue. Scattered dark granular material is also present.

The most likely diagnosis is a benign mesenchymal tumor composed of mature adipose tissue and smooth muscle, such as a lipoleiomyoma or a benign mesenchymal tumor with mixed differentiation.

Differential diagnoses include a leiomyoma with fatty change or a lipoma with smooth muscle metaplasia. The presence of both mature adipose tissue and smooth muscle bundles is key to the diagnosis.
tiles.rationale: Look for endometrial glands/stroma or pancreatic acini/ducts in more solid areas near cyst.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells with a wall composed of dense fibrous tissue. The lumen contains amorphous material and red blood cells.

The most likely diagnosis is a cystic lesion, such as a cystic lymphangioma or a simple cyst. Differential diagnoses include a cystic teratoma or a cystic mesothelioma, which would typically show more complex lining or additional tissue types.
tiles.rationale: High power of respiratory-type lining noted in right region; look for cilia, goblet cells, cartilage/seromucinous glands to support bronchogenic/foregut duplication cyst.

tiles.description:
The image shows a proliferation of spindle cells arranged in intersecting fascicles with abundant extravasated red blood cells. The spindle cells have elongated, blunt-ended nuclei and eosinophilic cytoplasm.

The most likely diagnosis is a leiomyoma. Differential diagnoses include gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) and schwannoma, which can be distinguished by immunohistochemistry (e.g., CD117, DOG1 for GIST; S100 for schwannoma).
tiles.rationale: Evaluate dark granular material/hemorrhage and vessel pattern; look for spindle cell lesion.

tiles.description:
The image shows a proliferation of spindle cells with elongated, wavy nuclei arranged in fascicles, interspersed with abundant extravasated red blood cells and scattered inflammatory cells. The spindle cells have eosinophilic cytoplasm and lack significant atypia or mitotic activity.

The most likely diagnosis is a benign mesenchymal tumor, such as a leiomyoma or a gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST).

Differential diagnoses include:
- Leiomyoma: Characterized by spindle cells with blunt-ended nuclei and eosinophilic cytoplasm, often arranged in intersecting fascicles. The absence of significant atypia and mitotic activity supports this.
- Gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST): Typically composed of spindle cells with variable cellularity and nuclear morphology. GISTs often show perivascular hyalinization and may have a more prominent inflammatory infiltrate. Immunohistochemistry for CD117 (c-KIT) and DOG1 would be helpful in distinguishing GIST from leiomyoma.
tiles.rationale: Deeper central area to assess for necrosis or different tumor morphology

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells with a wall composed of dense fibrous tissue and smooth muscle. There is also evidence of hemorrhage and inflammation in the surrounding tissue.

The most likely diagnosis is a cystic lesion of the gastrointestinal tract, such as a duplication cyst or a mesothelial cyst.

Differential diagnoses include a cystic teratoma, which would typically show elements from multiple germ layers, and a cystic lymphangioma, which would have a lining of endothelial cells.
tiles.rationale: Survey adjacent cyst wall for cartilage or bronchial glands; also assess smooth muscle layers.

tiles.description:
The image shows mature adipose tissue with areas of hemorrhage and inflammation. The adipocytes are uniform with no atypia.

The most likely diagnosis is a benign lipoma. Differential diagnoses include liposarcoma, which would show atypical adipocytes and lipoblasts, and inflammatory pseudotumor, which would have a more prominent inflammatory component and lack mature adipose tissue.
tiles.rationale: Assess smooth muscle + fat mixture: true tumor vs entrapped muscularis/adipose; look for capsule.

tiles.description:
The image shows a proliferation of spindle cells arranged in intersecting fascicles with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and elongated nuclei. There is also prominent hemorrhage and scattered inflammatory cells.

The most likely diagnosis is a smooth muscle tumor, such as a leiomyoma. Differential diagnoses include gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) and schwannoma, which can be distinguished by immunohistochemical staining for markers like CD117, DOG1, and S100.
tiles.rationale: Check for any solid tumor component separate from cyst wall (SFT/schwannoma/GIST) within region 0.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by columnar epithelium with underlying stroma containing a dense mixed inflammatory infiltrate, hemorrhage, and edema. The epithelial lining appears reactive.

The most likely diagnosis is a cystic lesion of the gastrointestinal tract, such as a cystic adenoma or a cystic change in a gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST).

Differential diagnoses include a cystic lymphangioma or a cystic teratoma, which would typically show different epithelial linings or additional tissue types, respectively.
tiles.rationale: Right-central solid area adjacent to cystic change for alternate pattern (staghorn, whorls)

tiles.description:
The image shows a proliferation of spindle cells arranged in intersecting fascicles with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and elongated nuclei. There is also prominent hemorrhage and scattered blood vessels.

The most likely diagnosis is a smooth muscle tumor, such as a leiomyoma. Differential diagnoses include gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) and schwannoma, which can be distinguished by immunohistochemical staining.
tiles.rationale: Look for endometrial glands/stroma/hemosiderin in hemorrhagic areas.

tiles.description:
The image shows a proliferation of spindle cells arranged in intersecting fascicles with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and elongated nuclei. There is also prominent hemorrhage and scattered inflammatory cells.

The most likely diagnosis is a smooth muscle tumor, such as a leiomyoma. Differential diagnoses include gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) and schwannoma, which can be distinguished by immunohistochemical staining for markers like CD117, DOG1, and S100.
tiles.rationale: Higher detail of presumed cyst lining to confirm epithelium type and any gastric/intestinal mucosa.

tiles.description:
The image shows bundles of smooth muscle fibers with elongated, blunt-ended nuclei and abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm, arranged in intersecting fascicles. There is also significant interstitial hemorrhage and scattered inflammatory cells.

The most likely diagnosis is a leiomyoma, a benign smooth muscle tumor. Differential diagnoses include gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST), which typically shows spindle cells with more prominent nucleoli and may have a different immunohistochemical profile (e.g., positive for CD117 and DOG1).
tiles.rationale: Higher detail of spindle fascicles to assess for palisading, perinuclear vacuoles, nuclear atypia, mitoses.

tiles.description:
The image shows a proliferation of spindle cells arranged in intersecting fascicles with elongated, blunt-ended nuclei and abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm. There is also prominent interstitial hemorrhage.

The most likely diagnosis is a leiomyoma. Differential diagnoses include gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) and schwannoma, which can be distinguished by immunohistochemistry (e.g., CD117, DOG1 for GIST; S100 for schwannoma).
tiles.rationale: Assess for staghorn vasculature/perivascular hyalinization/whorls at intermediate power in spindle area

tiles.description:
The image shows a proliferation of spindle cells arranged in intersecting fascicles with elongated, wavy nuclei and eosinophilic cytoplasm. There is also prominent hemorrhage and scattered inflammatory cells.

The most likely diagnosis is a benign mesenchymal tumor, such as a leiomyoma or schwannoma. Differential diagnoses include gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) and solitary fibrous tumor (SFT), which can be distinguished by immunohistochemical staining.
tiles.rationale: Intermixed fat/smooth muscle/fibrous tissue—assess whether resembles muscularis propria with serosal fat vs neoplasm.

tiles.description:
The image shows bundles of smooth muscle fibers interspersed with abundant extravasated red blood cells and scattered inflammatory cells. The smooth muscle bundles are arranged in a disorganized pattern.

The most likely diagnosis is a leiomyoma with hemorrhage. Differential diagnoses include a gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) and a leiomyosarcoma. GISTs typically show spindle cells with a more uniform appearance and may have a different immunohistochemical profile (e.g., CD117, DOG1 positive). Leiomyosarcomas would show increased cellularity, nuclear atypia, and mitotic activity.
tiles.rationale: Look for patternless pattern, collagen bands, or inflammatory cuffing

tiles.description:
The image shows a hypocellular lesion with abundant myxoid stroma containing scattered spindle cells and numerous small blood vessels. The stroma is loose and edematous with areas of hemorrhage.

The most likely diagnosis is a myxoma. Differential diagnoses include myxoid liposarcoma, which would show lipoblasts, and myxoid neurofibroma, which would have wavy nuclei and S100 positivity.
tiles.rationale: Right-sided fibrous area—look for staghorn vessels or solitary fibrous tumor pattern.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by columnar epithelium with underlying dense lymphoid tissue and smooth muscle. The surrounding stroma contains fibrous tissue, blood vessels, and scattered inflammatory cells.

The most likely diagnosis is a lymphoepithelial cyst. Differential diagnoses include a cystic teratoma, which would typically show additional tissue types such as squamous epithelium or cartilage, and a cystic lymphangioma, which would have a lining of flattened endothelial cells.
tiles.rationale: Evaluate cystic epithelial-lined area for glandular/endometrioid features vs mucosa

tiles.description:
The image shows mature adipose tissue with scattered small blood vessels. There is no evidence of lipoblasts or significant cellular atypia.

The most likely diagnosis is a lipoma. Differential diagnoses include well-differentiated liposarcoma, which would show lipoblasts and atypical stromal cells, and angiomyolipoma, which would contain smooth muscle and thick-walled blood vessels.
tiles.rationale: Check adipose fragment for hematopoietic elements (myelolipoma) or tumor infiltration

tiles.description:
The image shows bundles of elongated cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and blunt-ended nuclei, arranged in fascicles, with intervening hemorrhage and edema. These features are consistent with smooth muscle differentiation.

The most likely diagnosis is a leiomyoma, a benign smooth muscle tumor. Differential diagnoses include gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST), which typically shows spindle cells but often with more prominent nucleoli and may have a different immunohistochemical profile (e.g., positive for CD117 and DOG1).
tiles.rationale: Confirm smooth muscle cytology vs GIST/schwannoma; check mitoses/atypia/necrosis.

tiles.description:
The image shows bundles of smooth muscle cells with elongated, blunt-ended nuclei and eosinophilic cytoplasm, arranged in fascicles. There is also significant interstitial hemorrhage and edema.

The most likely diagnosis is a leiomyoma. Differential diagnoses include gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) and schwannoma, which can be distinguished by immunohistochemistry (e.g., CD117, DOG1 for GIST; S100 for schwannoma).
tiles.rationale: Another spindle-rich area; evaluate nuclear features, any perinuclear vacuoles, skeinoid fibers.

tiles.description:
The image shows a hypocellular lesion with abundant myxoid stroma containing scattered spindle cells and numerous small blood vessels. The spindle cells have elongated, wavy nuclei and are arranged in a loose pattern.

The most likely diagnosis is a myxoma. Differential diagnoses include myxoid neurofibroma, which would show more prominent wavy nuclei and potentially S100 positivity, and myxoid liposarcoma, which would contain lipoblasts and have a characteristic plexiform vascular pattern.
tiles.rationale: Hypocellular myxoid area—look for SFT patternless/staghorn vessels, inflammatory pseudotumor, or neurofibroma.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by flattened cells with a wall composed of dense fibrous tissue, smooth muscle, and adipose tissue. There is also evidence of hemorrhage and inflammation.

The most likely diagnosis is a cystic lesion of the left adrenal gland, such as an adrenal cyst or a cystic adrenal neoplasm.

Differential diagnoses include a cystic lesion of the stomach (e.g., gastric duplication cyst) or a cystic lesion of the pancreas (e.g., pseudocyst). The presence of smooth muscle and adipose tissue in the wall is more consistent with an adrenal origin.
tiles.rationale: Evaluate any cyst lining/epithelial elements vs entrapped serosa/mesothelium; look for endometrial glands.

tiles.description:
The image shows a disorganized proliferation of mature adipose tissue, smooth muscle, and fibrous tissue with prominent hemorrhage. The smooth muscle bundles are arranged in a haphazard pattern.

The most likely diagnosis is a mesenchymal hamartoma. This lesion is characterized by a disorganized mixture of mature mesenchymal tissues, including adipose tissue, smooth muscle, and fibrous tissue, often with hemorrhage. The haphazard arrangement of smooth muscle bundles is a key feature. Differential diagnoses include leiomyoma, which would show more organized smooth muscle bundles, and lipoma, which would be predominantly composed of mature adipose tissue.
tiles.rationale: Search for any residual GI wall (muscularis propria/submucosa) or nerve plexus to support gastric wall origin.

tiles.description:
The image shows a large deposit of amorphous, eosinophilic material with scattered inflammatory cells and hemorrhage, adjacent to gastric mucosa with foveolar hyperplasia and chronic inflammation.

The most likely diagnosis is amyloidosis.

Differential diagnoses include hyaline fibrosis or a hyalinized neoplasm. Amyloid is typically more amorphous and less fibrillar than hyaline fibrosis, and lacks the cellular components of a neoplasm.
tiles.rationale: Look for organoid/nested (zellballen) pattern suggesting paraganglioma.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cystic structure lined by gastric-type epithelium with underlying gastric glands and a wall composed of fibrous tissue, smooth muscle, and adipose tissue. The surrounding tissue includes pancreatic acini and ducts.

The most likely diagnosis is a foregut duplication cyst. The presence of gastric-type epithelium and underlying gastric glands within the wall of a cystic structure is characteristic of this entity. The surrounding pancreatic tissue suggests a possible origin in the foregut region. Differential diagnoses include a pancreatic pseudocyst, which typically lacks an epithelial lining, and a cystic teratoma, which would contain elements from multiple germ layers.
tiles.rationale: Scan for staghorn vessels/patternless architecture of SFT.

tiles.description:
The image shows a proliferation of spindle cells arranged in intersecting fascicles with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and elongated nuclei. There is also prominent hemorrhage and scattered inflammatory cells.

The most likely diagnosis is a smooth muscle tumor, such as a leiomyoma. Differential diagnoses include gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) and schwannoma, which can be distinguished by immunohistochemical staining for markers like CD117, DOG1, and S100.
tiles.rationale: Look for endometrial glands/stroma or hemosiderin-laden macrophages.

tiles.description:
The image shows a mixture of glandular structures, smooth muscle bundles, and fibrous stroma. The glands are lined by cuboidal to columnar epithelium, and the stroma contains scattered inflammatory cells and congested blood vessels.

The most likely diagnosis is a heterotopic gastric mucosa. This diagnosis is supported by the presence of glandular structures resembling gastric glands within a mass located in the left upper quadrant, attached to the stomach and adrenal gland. The presence of smooth muscle bundles and fibrous stroma is also consistent with this diagnosis.

Differential diagnoses to consider include a gastric duplication cyst or a gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST). A gastric duplication cyst would typically show a more organized cystic structure lined by gastric-type epithelium, often with a muscular wall. A GIST would be characterized by a proliferation of spindle cells or epithelioid cells, often with a prominent vascular network, and would typically lack the glandular component seen here.
tiles.rationale: Confirm gastric-type mucosa/glands lining cyst and presence of pancreatic tissue; assess for muscular wall typical of duplication cyst.

tiles.description:
The image shows a mixture of glandular structures, smooth muscle bundles, and fibrous stroma with a prominent inflammatory infiltrate. The glandular epithelium is columnar with basally oriented nuclei and apical mucin.

The most likely diagnosis is a heterotopic gastric mucosa (gastric heterotopia) with associated smooth muscle and fibrous tissue. The presence of gastric-type glands, smooth muscle, and fibrous tissue in an ectopic location supports this diagnosis. Differential diagnoses include a leiomyoma with entrapped glands or a gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) with entrapped glands, although the glandular morphology is more consistent with gastric mucosa.
tiles.rationale: High-power confirmation of epithelial lining type (foveolar, ciliated, squamous) and gastric glands; rule out dysplasia.

tiles.description:
The image shows a mass composed of large cells with abundant eosinophilic granular cytoplasm and small, round nuclei, arranged in nests and cords. There is also a prominent inflammatory infiltrate and areas of hemorrhage.

The most likely diagnosis is a granular cell tumor. Differential diagnoses include oncocytoma, which typically shows more uniform cells with less granular cytoplasm, and metastatic renal cell carcinoma, which often has clear cytoplasm and a different architectural pattern.
tiles.rationale: Assess eosinophilic amorphous material vs mucin/necrosis; relation to mucosa.

tiles.description:
The image shows a cellular neoplasm with a fibrillary background, composed of large cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and eccentric nuclei, along with smaller cells and areas of hemorrhage.

The most likely diagnosis is a gangliocytoma, a benign tumor composed of mature ganglion cells.

Differential diagnoses include ganglioglioma, which would show a glial component, and other neuroendocrine tumors, which would typically have different cellular morphology and immunohistochemical profiles.
tiles.rationale: HP of amorphous material for amyloid-like quality vs fibrin, collagen.
